title: Why Shakespeare Works Best When Acted Out
subtitle: 莎士比亚为什么要演出来？一项教学实验的发现

paragraphs.en: The grammar school boy from Stratford-upon-Avon has landed a scholarly punch after groundbreaking research showed that Shakespeare does benefit children's literacy and emotional development. But only if you act him out. A study found that a "rehearsal room" approach to teaching Shakespeare broadened children's vocabulary and the complexity of their writing as well as their emotional literacy.
paragraphs.zh: 这位来自埃文河畔斯特拉特福的文法学校学生，如今在学术上再添有力一击——一项开创性研究表明，莎士比亚确实有助于提升儿童的读写能力与情感发展。但前提是，你得把他演出来。一项研究发现，采用“排练室”方式教授莎士比亚，能拓宽儿童的词汇量、提高其写作的复杂度，并增强其情感素养。

paragraphs.en: "The research shows that the way actors work makes a big difference to the way children use language and also how they think about themselves," said Jacqui O'Hanlon of the Royal Shakespeare Company, which commissioned the study. The randomised control trial involved hundreds of year 5 pupils — aged nine and ten — at 45 state primary schools that had not been "previously exposed to RSC pedagogy." They were split into target and control groups and asked to write, for example, a message in a bottle as Ferdinand following the shipwreck in The Tempest.
paragraphs.zh: 委托进行这项研究的皇家莎士比亚剧团负责人雅基·奥汉隆表示：“研究表明，演员的工作方式对儿童运用语言的方式以及他们如何看待自我，均有显著影响。”这项随机对照试验涉及45所公立小学的数百名五年级学生（年龄在九到十岁之间），这些学校此前均未接触过皇家莎士比亚剧团的教学法。学生被分为目标组和对照组，并被要求完成写作任务，例如，以《暴风雨》中遭遇沉船后的斐迪南的口吻，写一封瓶中信。

paragraphs.en: The target group were given a 30-minute drama-based activity to accompany the passage. The peer-reviewed results showed that the target group of pupils drew on a wider vocabulary, used words "classed as more sophisticated or rarer", and wrote at greater length. They also "appear to be more comfortable writing in role. While control pupils imagine how they themselves would react to being shipwrecked, target children put themselves in the shoes of a literary character and express that character's emotion."
paragraphs.zh: 目标组学生配合该文段进行了30分钟的戏剧活动。经过同行评审的结果显示，目标组学生运用的词汇更广泛，使用了“更复杂或更罕见的”词语，且写作篇幅更长。他们还“显得更适应以角色身份写作。对照组学生想象的是自己遭遇沉船会作何反应，而目标组儿童则将自己代入文学人物的立场，并表达该人物的情感”。

paragraphs.en: The Time to Act study, which is published by the RSC this week, also found that while control pupils relied on "desert island clichés" such as palm trees, target pupils were "more expansive giving a broader picture of the sky, the sea and the atmospheric conditions." O'Hanlon said she had been most surprised by the "emotional literacy that was evident in the target children's writing" and that they were "more resilient in their writing, more hopeful."
paragraphs.zh: 皇家莎士比亚剧团本周发表的《行动时刻》研究报告还发现，对照组学生的写作依赖于棕榈树等“荒岛陈词滥调”，而目标组学生则“更开阔，描绘出天空、海洋及大气状况的更广阔图景”。奥汉隆表示，最令她惊讶的是“目标组儿童写作中明显流露的情感素养”，以及他们“在写作中更具韧性、更充满希望”。

paragraphs.en: She added: "The emotional understanding was very evident and it is probably related to the rehearsal room process where you are used to trying to imagine your way through. They were comfortable in describing different emotional states and part of what you do in drama is put yourself in different shoes." The study showed the importance of embedding arts in education, she said.
paragraphs.zh: 她补充道：“情感理解能力非常明显，这很可能与排练室的过程有关——在那里你习惯于设身处地去想象。他们能够自如地描述不同的情感状态，而戏剧活动的一部分内容恰恰就是换位思考。”她说，这项研究体现了将艺术融入教育的重要性。

paragraphs.en: But could the results be replicated with any old dramatist? O'Hanlon said more research would be needed but suggested that Shakespeare's use of 20,000 words, compared with the everyday 2,000 words, gave a "massive expansion of language into children's lives", which was combined with children "using their whole bodies to bring words to life."
paragraphs.zh: 但是，换成任何一位老剧作家，结果都能复现吗？奥汉隆表示还需要更多研究，但她指出，莎士比亚使用了约两万个词汇，而日常用词仅两千左右，这为“儿童的生活带来了语言的大规模扩展”，再加上孩子们“用整个身体让词语活了起来”，两者相辅相成。
